report_metadata.cancer_type: Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC) of the lung
molecular_profile: SCLC-like molecular subtype (inferred from RB1 loss)

treatments.name: Platinum-Etoposide + Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor (Atezolizumab/PD-1 Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Retrospective real-world study (n=31) showed significant OS/PFS benefit. Extrapolated from IMpower133 Phase 3 in SCLC. ESMO 2023 poster on atezolizumab+PE in metastatic LCNEC. No prospective Phase 3 in LCNEC specifically, but strong biological rationale

survival_benefit.rationale: ICIs+chemo: median OS 15.0 months vs 11.0 months chemo alone (p=0.036). Median PFS 10.5 vs 6.0 months (p=0.035). ORR 50% vs 29.4%. DCR 85.7% vs 76.5%. 49% reduction in progression risk (HR=0.51). Meaningful survival improvement over chemo alone

accessibility.rationale: Atezolizumab FDA-approved for ES-SCLC (IMpower133). Off-label use for LCNEC requires oncologist discretion. Increasingly adopted at academic centers. Insurance coverage may require prior authorization for LCNEC indication

safety_profile.rationale: In the LCNEC retrospective study, immune-related AEs were grade 1-2 only with no grade 3+ events observed. Favorable safety profile compared to chemo alone. Standard chemotherapy side effects plus manageable irAEs

biomarker_match.rationale: LCNEC has median TMB of 9.9 mut/Mb supporting immunotherapy benefit. PD-L1 positive in 16% of stage IV LCNEC. RB1 loss/SCLC-like biology supports extrapolation from IMpower133. High Ki-67 indicates immunogenicity from rapid proliferation

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Platinum-etoposide chemotherapy induces immunogenic cell death releasing tumor neoantigens, while atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1) or PD-1 inhibitors block immune checkpoint pathways, preventing tumor immune evasion. The combination converts immunologically cold tumors to hot and unleashes T-cell-mediated anti-tumor responses.

key_evidence.study_name: First-line ICI + chemotherapy in advanced LCNEC (Frontiers in Oncology 2025)

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 expression may enhance benefit (not required)
treatments.availability: Off-label for LCNEC — atezolizumab FDA-approved for ES-SCLC (October 2019). Increasingly used at academic centers

treatments.name: Cisplatin/Carboplatin + Etoposide (Platinum-Etoposide)

evidence_level.rationale: No Phase 3 RCTs specific to LCNEC due to rarity. Supported by multiple retrospective studies and extrapolation from extensive SCLC Phase 3 data. NCCN recommends treatment per SCLC guidelines for SCLC-like LCNEC. Molecular subtyping study (Rekhtman et al., CCR 2018) supports platinum-etoposide for RB1-mutant SCLC-like LCNEC

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS 8-16.5 months in advanced LCNEC. Cisplatin-etoposide showed median OS 16.5 months in one retrospective analysis. Substantially less chemo-responsive than SCLC. ORR approximately 40-50% for SCLC-like LCNEC subtypes

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved generic chemotherapy agents available worldwide. Standard first-line regimen at all oncology centers. No specialized testing required beyond histological diagnosis

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized toxicity profile from decades of SCLC use. Significant myelosuppression, nausea/vomiting, nephrotoxicity (cisplatin), neurotoxicity. Manageable with supportive care in ECOG 1 patient

biomarker_match.rationale: RB1 loss classifies this tumor as SCLC-like subtype, which has been shown to respond preferentially to platinum-etoposide over NSCLC-type regimens (Rekhtman et al.). High Ki-67 confirms high-grade neuroendocrine biology consistent with platinum-etoposide sensitivity

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin/carboplatin cross-links DNA strands, preventing DNA replication and transcription. Etoposide inhibits topoisomerase II, causing double-strand DNA breaks. The combination produces synergistic cytotoxicity against rapidly proliferating neuroendocrine tumor cells with high Ki-67 index.

key_evidence.study_name: Molecular Subtypes of Pulmonary LCNEC Predict Chemotherapy Outcome (Rekhtman et al.)

treatments.availability: FDA Approved — standard first-line chemotherapy for SCLC-like LCNEC per NCCN guidelines

treatments.name: Tarlatamab (IMDELLTRA) — DLL3/CD3 Bispecific T-Cell Engager

evidence_level.rationale: FDA full approval for ES-SCLC (November 2025) based on DeLLphi-304 Phase 3. DLL3 expression in 74% of stage IV LCNEC supports biological rationale. No LCNEC-specific Phase 3 but strong mechanistic basis given shared DLL3 biology

survival_benefit.rationale: In SCLC: median OS 13.6 months vs 8.3 months SOC (DeLLphi-304). PFS 4.2 vs 3.2 months. ORR 55% in Phase 2 (DeLLphi-301). Duration of response 12.3 months. Extrapolated benefit for DLL3+ LCNEC expected to be significant

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for ES-SCLC since November 2025. Off-label use for LCNEC requires DLL3 testing and oncologist advocacy. Available at major cancer centers. Insurance coverage for LCNEC may be challenging

safety_profile.rationale: Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) is the primary safety concern. Step-up dosing mitigates CRS risk. Immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS) possible. Requires inpatient monitoring for initial doses

biomarker_match.rationale: DLL3 expressed in 74% of stage IV LCNEC, linked to ASCL1 and neuroendocrine markers. RB1 loss associated with neuroendocrine differentiation correlates with DLL3 expression. Requires DLL3 IHC confirmation

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Tarlatamab is a first-in-class bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE) that simultaneously binds DLL3 on tumor cells and CD3 on T cells, creating an immunological synapse that redirects cytotoxic T cells to kill DLL3-expressing cancer cells. DLL3 is a Notch ligand highly expressed on neuroendocrine tumors but minimally on normal tissues.

key_evidence.study_name: DeLLphi-304 Phase 3 (Tarlatamab vs SOC in Relapsed ES-SCLC)

biomarker_requirements: DLL3 expression by IHC (high expression preferred)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for ES-SCLC (November 2025). Off-label for DLL3+ LCNEC — available at specialized centers

treatments.name: Lurbinectedin + Atezolizumab (Maintenance after Platinum-Etoposide Induction)

evidence_level.rationale: FDA approved (October 2025) for ES-SCLC maintenance based on IMforte Phase 3 (n=534). No LCNEC-specific data but strong rationale from shared high-grade neuroendocrine biology with SCLC

survival_benefit.rationale: IMforte: median OS 13.2 months vs 10.6 months (atezolizumab alone). PFS 5.4 vs 2.1 months. Significant improvement in maintenance setting. Applicable to LCNEC patients who achieve disease control after platinum-etoposide induction

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for ES-SCLC maintenance (October 2025). Both lurbinectedin and atezolizumab individually available. Off-label for LCNEC requires oncologist justification. Available at most cancer centers

safety_profile.rationale: Lurbinectedin: myelosuppression, fatigue, nausea. Combined with atezolizumab irAEs. Manageable toxicity in maintenance setting. LUPER study confirmed tolerability of lurbinectedin + pembrolizumab

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets high-grade neuroendocrine biology shared between SCLC and SCLC-like LCNEC. RB1 loss supports SCLC-like phenotype. Not biomarker-dependent but neuroendocrine histology preferred

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lurbinectedin binds to the minor groove of DNA and inhibits oncogenic transcription by targeting RNA polymerase II, leading to tumor cell apoptosis. It also modifies the tumor microenvironment by depleting tumor-associated macrophages. Combined with atezolizumab anti-PD-L1, it creates synergistic anti-tumor immunity.

key_evidence.study_name: IMforte Phase 3 Trial (Lurbinectedin + Atezolizumab Maintenance in ES-SCLC)

treatments.availability: FDA Approved for ES-SCLC maintenance (October 2025). Off-label for LCNEC

treatments.name: Cisplatin + Irinotecan

evidence_level.rationale: Prospective Phase 2 trial by Niho et al. specifically in LCNEC (n=44). One of the few prospective trials in LCNEC. Also established as SCLC first-line alternative in Japanese studies

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 40% in LCNEC (vs 80% in SCLC), median OS 12.6 months in the LCNEC cohort. Comparable to platinum-etoposide but with different toxicity profile. May be preferred for certain patients

accessibility.rationale: Both components are widely available generic chemotherapeutics. Standard alternative regimen. Available at all oncology centers worldwide

safety_profile.rationale: Diarrhea is the dose-limiting toxicity with irinotecan (more prominent than with etoposide). Myelosuppression, nausea/vomiting. Cisplatin nephrotoxicity. UGT1A1 polymorphism affects irinotecan metabolism

biomarker_match.rationale: Applicable to SCLC-like LCNEC with RB1 loss. Prospective trial data directly in LCNEC population. Not biomarker-selected but appropriate for neuroendocrine histology

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin forms DNA cross-links causing strand breaks and apoptosis. Irinotecan is a topoisomerase I inhibitor that prevents DNA religation during replication, causing lethal single-strand breaks. The combination provides complementary DNA-damaging mechanisms effective against rapidly dividing neuroendocrine tumors.

key_evidence.study_name: Cisplatin-Irinotecan Phase 2 in LCNEC (Niho et al.)

treatments.availability: FDA Approved — widely available first-line alternative for LCNEC

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab Monotherapy (Checkpoint Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Small case series (approximately 30+ patients total across multiple reports) showing responses in platinum-refractory LCNEC. 60% partial response rate in one pretreated series (Levra et al.). No dedicated Phase 3 for LCNEC

survival_benefit.rationale: 60% PR rate in pretreated LCNEC in one series. PD-L1+ LCNEC patients show superior OS. TMB 9.9 mut/Mb supports immunotherapy potential. Limited mature survival data

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for multiple indications including NSCLC. TMB-high pan-tumor approval enables use for LCNEC with TMB ≥10. Widely available globally

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized immune-related AE profile. Generally well-tolerated monotherapy. irAEs manageable with standard algorithms. Favorable compared to chemotherapy toxicity

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 positive in 16% of stage IV LCNEC — benefit enriched in PD-L1+ tumors. TMB-high approval pathway available. RB1 loss not directly predictive of ICI response but neuroendocrine biology may support immunogenicity

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab is a humanized IgG4 anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that blocks the interaction between PD-1 on T cells and PD-L1/PD-L2 on tumor cells, reactivating T-cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity. Removes the immune checkpoint brake allowing cytotoxic T cells to recognize and destroy tumor cells.

key_evidence.study_name: Checkpoint Inhibitors in Pretreated LCNEC (Levra et al. and case series)
key_evidence.journal: Multiple (Lung Cancer, Journal of Thoracic Oncology)

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 expression (TPS ≥1% preferred, benefit enriched at ≥50%)
TMB-high (≥10 mut/Mb) enables pan-tumor FDA approval
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — available for LCNEC under TMB-high pan-tumor or off-label use

treatments.name: Carboplatin + Paclitaxel + Everolimus (mTOR Inhibitor Combination)

evidence_level.rationale: Multicenter Phase 2 trial specifically in metastatic LCNEC (Christopoulos et al.). One of the few prospective combination trials in LCNEC. ORR 45% and median OS 9.9 months reported

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 45% with median OS 9.9 months. Modest survival benefit comparable to standard platinum-etoposide. mTOR pathway activation is frequent in neuroendocrine tumors providing biological rationale

accessibility.rationale: All three components are individually FDA-approved. Everolimus approved for pancreatic NET and lung carcinoid. Off-label combination for LCNEC feasible at academic centers

safety_profile.rationale: Triple combination increases toxicity. Everolimus adds mucositis, hyperglycemia, pneumonitis risk. Combined myelosuppression from carboplatin/paclitaxel. Manageable but requires careful monitoring

biomarker_match.rationale: mTOR pathway frequently activated in neuroendocrine tumors. Not specifically selected by RB1 loss but relevant to neuroendocrine biology. High Ki-67 tumors may have enhanced mTOR pathway dependence

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Carboplatin and paclitaxel provide cytotoxic backbone through DNA cross-linking and microtubule stabilization respectively. Everolimus inhibits mTOR (mammalian target of rapamycin), blocking a key survival signaling pathway frequently overactivated in neuroendocrine tumors, reducing cellular proliferation and angiogenesis.

key_evidence.study_name: Everolimus + Paclitaxel + Carboplatin Phase 2 in Metastatic LCNEC (Christopoulos et al.)

biomarker_requirements: mTOR pathway activation (preferred but not required)
treatments.availability: Off-label combination — all components individually FDA-approved. Phase 2 data supports use

treatments.name: Obrixtamig (BI 764532) — DLL3/CD3 T-Cell Engager

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1 dose-escalation trial with LCNEC-specific cohort data presented at ASCO 2025. ORR 54% and DCR 77% in LCNEC reported. Early but promising data specifically in LCNEC (not just extrapolated from SCLC)

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 54% in LCNEC and DCR 77% are remarkably high for a pretreated rare tumor. In DLL3-high epNEC: ORR 40%, median DOR 7.9 months. Potential for meaningful survival extension in DLL3+ LCNEC

accessibility.rationale: Investigational — available only through clinical trials. Phase 1 expansion ongoing. Boehringer Ingelheim development. Limited trial sites

safety_profile.rationale: IgG-like format may have lower CRS risk than BiTE antibodies. T-cell engager class effects (CRS, ICANS) expected but manageable with step-up dosing. Early safety data acceptable

biomarker_match.rationale: Specifically targets DLL3, expressed in 74% of LCNEC. RB1 loss in this patient correlates with neuroendocrine differentiation and higher DLL3 expression. LCNEC-specific efficacy data available (unlike tarlatamab)

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Obrixtamig is a DLL3/CD3 IgG-like T-cell engager with a longer half-life than BiTE antibodies, enabling less frequent dosing. It redirects endogenous T cells to kill DLL3-expressing tumor cells through formation of immunological synapses, inducing perforin/granzyme-mediated tumor lysis.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase 1 BI 764532 in DLL3+ SCLC and NEC (ASCO 2025)

biomarker_requirements: DLL3 expression by IHC (high expression preferred for best response)
treatments.availability: Clinical trial only — Phase 1 expansion (Boehringer Ingelheim)

treatments.name: Topotecan (Second-Line Monotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Established second-line therapy for SCLC with extensive Phase 3 data. Extrapolated to LCNEC per SCLC treatment guidelines. RESILIENT Phase 3 confirmed topotecan as standard comparator in relapsed SCLC

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS 8.3 months in relapsed SCLC (RESILIENT). LCNEC second-line: median PFS 3.3 months, median OS 8.3 months. Modest but established benefit in platinum-refractory setting

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved generic drug available worldwide. Standard second-line option. No biomarker testing required. Available at all oncology centers

safety_profile.rationale: Significant myelosuppression, especially neutropenia. Grade 3-4 hematologic toxicity common. Fatigue, nausea, diarrhea. Dose reductions frequently needed. Challenging for ECOG >1 patients

biomarker_match.rationale: Applicable to SCLC-like LCNEC as standard second-line. RB1 loss supports SCLC-like treatment paradigm. Not biomarker-specific

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Topotecan is a topoisomerase I inhibitor that binds the topoisomerase I-DNA complex, preventing religation of single-strand DNA breaks during replication, leading to double-strand breaks and apoptosis in rapidly dividing tumor cells.

key_evidence.study_name: RESILIENT Phase 3: Liposomal Irinotecan vs Topotecan in Relapsed SCLC

treatments.availability: FDA Approved — standard second-line therapy for high-grade neuroendocrine carcinomas

treatments.name: Capecitabine + Temozolomide (CAPTEM)

evidence_level.rationale: Limited data in lung neuroendocrine neoplasms. ORR 30% in one study of advanced lung NETs. Well-established in GI neuroendocrine tumors. Mostly retrospective data

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 30% in advanced lung NETs with relatively durable responses. Primarily studied in carcinoid/well-differentiated NETs. Efficacy in high-grade LCNEC less certain

accessibility.rationale: Both oral agents are widely available and FDA-approved for other indications. Can be administered outpatient. Low logistic burden

safety_profile.rationale: Oral regimen with relatively mild toxicity profile. Hand-foot syndrome (capecitabine), myelosuppression (temozolomide), nausea. Well-tolerated compared to IV chemotherapy

biomarker_match.rationale: Better established for low/intermediate-grade neuroendocrine tumors. MGMT promoter methylation may predict temozolomide benefit. RB1 loss and high Ki-67 suggest aggressive biology where CAPTEM may be less effective than platinum

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Temozolomide is an oral alkylating agent that methylates DNA at O6-guanine, causing mismatch repair-mediated cell death. Capecitabine is an oral fluoropyrimidine prodrug converted to 5-FU in tumor tissue, inhibiting thymidylate synthase and DNA synthesis. The combination provides synergistic DNA damage.

key_evidence.study_name: CAPTEM in Advanced Lung Neuroendocrine Neoplasms

biomarker_requirements: MGMT promoter methylation (predictive of temozolomide benefit)
treatments.availability: Off-label — both components FDA-approved for other indications. Oral regimen

treatments.name: Amrubicin (Second-Line — Available in Japan)

evidence_level.rationale: Second-line data in LCNEC (n=18) showing ORR 27.7%. Most common second-line regimen in Japanese LCNEC studies. Phase 3 data in SCLC. Not approved in Western countries

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 27.7% in pretreated LCNEC. Median PFS 3.3 months and OS 8.3 months for LCNEC second-line. Active topoisomerase II inhibitor with different mechanism from etoposide

accessibility.rationale: Approved only in Japan. Not available in the US or Europe. Would require medical tourism or compassionate use. Significant geographic limitation

safety_profile.rationale: Significant myelosuppression, particularly febrile neutropenia. Cardiotoxicity risk (anthracycline class). Requires cardiac monitoring. Manageable with G-CSF support

biomarker_match.rationale: Applicable to SCLC-like LCNEC regardless of specific biomarkers. RB1 loss does not specifically predict amrubicin benefit. Universal applicability within neuroendocrine histology

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Amrubicin is a fully synthetic 9-amino-anthracycline that is converted to its active metabolite amrubicinol, a potent topoisomerase II inhibitor. Unlike etoposide, it produces DNA strand breaks through a different binding mechanism, offering non-cross-resistant activity in platinum-etoposide-treated patients.
treatments.availability: Approved in Japan only. Not available in the US/EU

treatments.name: Anlotinib (Multi-Target TKI — Anti-Angiogenic)

evidence_level.rationale: Approved in China for third-line SCLC. Phase 2/3 data in SCLC showing OS and PFS improvement. Limited LCNEC-specific data. Extrapolated from SCLC and NSCLC studies

survival_benefit.rationale: Demonstrated OS and PFS improvement as third-line therapy in SCLC in Chinese patients. Anti-angiogenic activity may provide disease stabilization. Modest monotherapy activity expected in LCNEC

accessibility.rationale: Approved in China for third-line SCLC. Not approved in US or Europe. Available through clinical trials or international procurement. Geographic limitation

safety_profile.rationale: Oral TKI with generally manageable toxicity. Hypertension, fatigue, hand-foot syndrome. Well-tolerated compared to cytotoxic chemotherapy. Suitable for maintenance or later-line settings

biomarker_match.rationale: Anti-angiogenic mechanism broadly applicable. Not specifically selected by RB1 loss. VEGFR pathway targeting relevant but not specific to neuroendocrine biology

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Anlotinib is a multi-target tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, PDGFR-alpha/beta, and c-Kit. It inhibits tumor angiogenesis by blocking multiple pro-angiogenic pathways and directly inhibits tumor cell proliferation through receptor tyrosine kinase suppression.

key_evidence.study_name: ALTER 1202: Anlotinib as Third-Line Therapy in SCLC

treatments.availability: Approved in China for third-line SCLC. Not available in US/EU outside clinical trials

treatments.name: ZL-1310 (Next-Generation DLL3-Targeting ADC)

evidence_level.rationale: Early Phase 1/2 clinical development. Novel DLL3-targeting antibody-drug conjugate on TMALIN ADC platform. Regulatory submission targeted for 2026. Limited published efficacy data

survival_benefit.rationale: Positioned to address limited survival gains in high-grade neuroendocrine tumors. Preclinical data demonstrates tumor eradication of DLL3+ neuroendocrine tumor-initiating cells. Mature clinical data pending

accessibility.rationale: Investigational — Phase 1/2 clinical trials only. Regulatory submission estimated 2026, potential approval 2028. Very limited access currently

safety_profile.rationale: ADC platform designed for manageable toxicity. Novel TMALIN linker technology may improve therapeutic index. Early safety data suggests tolerability. ADC class effects (hematologic, ocular, neuropathy) expected

biomarker_match.rationale: DLL3 expressed in 74% of LCNEC. RB1 loss correlates with neuroendocrine differentiation and DLL3 expression. ADC may be effective regardless of immune microenvironment status

treatments.mechanism_of_action: ZL-1310 is a next-generation antibody-drug conjugate targeting DLL3. It uses the TMALIN ADC platform to deliver a cytotoxic payload specifically to DLL3-expressing tumor cells. Upon internalization, the linker releases the payload intracellularly, causing targeted cell death while sparing DLL3-negative normal tissues.
treatments.availability: Clinical trial only — regulatory submission targeted 2026, potential approval 2028

treatments.name: 225Ac-ABD147 (Targeted Radionuclide Therapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Early-phase clinical trial for SCLC and LCNEC after platinum-based chemotherapy. Novel targeted alpha-emitter approach. Limited published clinical data

survival_benefit.rationale: Targeted alpha-particle therapy delivers highly cytotoxic radiation directly to tumor cells. Potential for durable responses based on radionuclide therapy precedent in other cancers. Efficacy data pending

accessibility.rationale: Available only at select academic centers (UCSF, UCLA). Requires specialized nuclear medicine infrastructure. Early clinical trial access only

safety_profile.rationale: Alpha-emitter radionuclide therapy carries risk of renal toxicity, xerostomia, myelosuppression. Short-range alpha particles limit off-target damage. Safety profile being established

biomarker_match.rationale: Designed for high-grade neuroendocrine carcinomas including LCNEC. Target antigen specifics pending. RB1 loss not specifically predictive but neuroendocrine differentiation required

treatments.mechanism_of_action: 225Ac-ABD147 is a targeted alpha-particle emitting radionuclide therapy conjugated to an antibody or peptide targeting a neuroendocrine tumor-specific antigen. Actinium-225 emits high-energy alpha particles that cause irreparable double-strand DNA breaks within a short range (50-80 micrometers), selectively destroying tumor cells while minimizing damage to surrounding tissue.

key_evidence.study_name: 225Ac-ABD147 in SCLC and LCNEC after Platinum-Based Chemotherapy

treatments.availability: Clinical trial only — select academic centers (UCSF, UCLA)

treatments.name: BL-M14D1 (Novel Agent for NEC)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1 trial (NCT07080242) began enrolling April 2025. First-in-human for SCLC, LCNEC, and other NETs. No efficacy data available yet

survival_benefit.rationale: No clinical efficacy data available. Early Phase 1 for safety and dose-finding. Potential benefit unknown pending results

accessibility.rationale: Phase 1 clinical trial only. Very limited enrollment sites. Early development stage. Years from potential approval

safety_profile.rationale: No safety data published yet. Phase 1 dose-escalation will establish safety profile. Unknown toxicity profile

biomarker_match.rationale: Enrolling LCNEC specifically alongside SCLC and other NETs. Applicable to the patient's tumor type. Specific biomarker requirements pending

treatments.mechanism_of_action: BL-M14D1 is a novel investigational agent being evaluated for advanced SCLC, large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC), and other neuroendocrine tumors. Specific mechanism of action details are limited at this early stage of clinical development.

key_evidence.study_name: BL-M14D1 Phase 1 in Advanced SCLC and Neuroendocrine Tumors (NCT07080242)

biomarker_requirements: Advanced/metastatic SCLC, LCNEC, or other NET
Failed at least 1 line of standard therapy
notable_side_effects: To be determined — Phase 1 dose-escalation ongoing
treatments.availability: Phase 1 clinical trial only (NCT07080242) — enrolled since April 2025

clinical_trials.title: BL-M14D1 for Advanced SCLC, LCNEC, and Other Neuroendocrine Tumors
biomarker_requirements: Advanced/metastatic SCLC, LCNEC, or other NET
clinical_trials.early_results: Enrollment began April 2025. No efficacy data reported yet. First-in-human dose-escalation study

clinical_trials.title: Phase 1 Dose-Escalation of Obrixtamig (BI 764532) in DLL3+ SCLC and Neuroendocrine Carcinomas
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 54% in LCNEC cohort, DCR 77%. ORR 40% in DLL3-high extrapulmonary NEC, median DOR 7.9 months. Presented at ASCO 2025

clinical_trials.title: 225Ac-ABD147 Targeted Alpha-Particle Therapy in SCLC and LCNEC Post-Platinum
clinical_trials.early_results: Early-phase safety and efficacy data collection ongoing. Targeted radionuclide approach for neuroendocrine tumors

clinical_trials.title: MK-6070/HPN328 — DLL3-Targeted Therapy for Advanced DLL3-Expressing Cancers
clinical_trials.early_results: Evaluating DLL3-targeting agents in SCLC and neuroendocrine carcinomas including LCNEC. Multiple DLL3-directed candidates under investigation

clinical_trials.title: ZL-1310 DLL3-Targeting ADC in DLL3+ High-Grade Neuroendocrine Tumors
clinical_trials.early_results: Next-generation DLL3-targeting ADC on TMALIN platform. Regulatory submission targeted 2026. Designed to improve on prior DLL3 ADC (rovalpituzumab) limitations

clinical_trials.title: Single-Arm Trial of Atezolizumab/Platinum/Etoposide for Advanced LCNEC
clinical_trials.early_results: Prospective evaluation of IMpower133 regimen in LCNEC specifically. Data collection ongoing

clinical_trials.title: Lurbinectedin + Immunotherapy Combinations in High-Grade Neuroendocrine Tumors
clinical_trials.early_results: LUPER study (lurbinectedin + pembrolizumab) in relapsed SCLC showed feasibility. Exploring combinations in broader NEC population including LCNEC

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Phase 3 in SCLC (IMpower133) / Retrospective in LCNEC
combination_strategies.rationale: Standard platinum-etoposide backbone with immunotherapy augmentation. In LCNEC: OS 15.0 vs 11.0 months (p=0.036), PFS 10.5 vs 6.0 months, ORR 50% vs 29%. Chemotherapy-induced immunogenic cell death synergizes with PD-L1 blockade. RB1 loss/SCLC-like biology supports IMpower133 extrapolation. Emerging standard of care for advanced LCNEC

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Phase 3 (IMforte in SCLC) / FDA Approved for ES-SCLC
combination_strategies.rationale: After platinum-etoposide induction, lurbinectedin depletes tumor-associated macrophages and modifies the tumor microenvironment, enhancing atezolizumab anti-PD-L1 efficacy. OS 13.2 vs 10.6 months in SCLC maintenance. Applicable to LCNEC patients achieving disease control after induction. Novel maintenance strategy for high-grade neuroendocrine biology

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Phase 2 (Christopoulos et al., specifically in LCNEC)
combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy cytotoxicity combined with mTOR pathway inhibition. LCNEC-specific Phase 2: ORR 45%, median OS 9.9 months. mTOR pathway frequently overactivated in neuroendocrine tumors. Everolimus FDA-approved for pancreatic NET and lung carcinoid. Provides alternative combination when platinum-etoposide is not preferred

Tarlatamab redirects T cells to DLL3+ tumor cells while chemotherapy reduces tumor burden and induces immunogenic cell death, potentially enhancing BiTE activity. Sequential or concurrent use could maximize DLL3-directed killing in DLL3+ LCNEC. Active investigation in SCLC with potential LCNEC expansion. RB1 loss correlates with DLL3 expression

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Retrospective series / Extrapolated from SCLC Phase 3
combination_strategies.rationale: Brain metastasis incidence in LCNEC is 21.4% at 1 year and 41.3% at 2 years — higher than typical NSCLC. PCI significantly reduces brain metastasis risk in SCLC (NEJM Phase 3). Small LCNEC series showed survival improvement with PCI in stage III-IV. Controversial but worth discussing given high brain metastasis risk

supportive_care.evidence: Temel et al. (NEJM 2010) demonstrated 2.7-month OS improvement with early palliative care in metastatic lung cancer. ASCO/NCCN guidelines strongly recommend integration. LCNEC median OS 7-16 months in stage IV makes early palliative care essential
supportive_care.benefit: Improved quality of life, symptom management, advance care planning, reduced ER visits and hospitalizations. May extend survival by optimizing symptom control and treatment tolerance. Critical given aggressive disease course of stage IVB LCNEC

supportive_care.evidence: ASCO/EORTC guidelines for febrile neutropenia prophylaxis. Platinum-etoposide and topotecan carry >20% febrile neutropenia risk. NCCN recommends primary G-CSF prophylaxis for high-risk chemotherapy regimens
supportive_care.benefit: Reduces febrile neutropenia risk by 50-60%, prevents chemotherapy dose reductions and delays, maintains treatment intensity critical for aggressive LCNEC. Enables optimal dose-density of platinum-etoposide regimen. Pegfilgrastim or biosimilar administration day after each chemotherapy cycle

supportive_care.approach: Brain MRI Surveillance for Early Metastasis Detection
supportive_care.evidence: Brain metastasis incidence 21.4% at 1 year and 41.3% at 2 years in LCNEC — significantly higher than typical NSCLC. NCCN recommends brain MRI at baseline and every 3-4 cycles for high-grade neuroendocrine carcinomas
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of brain metastases enables timely intervention with stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) or whole-brain radiation therapy (WBRT). Prevents neurological catastrophe from undetected CNS progression. Informs eligibility for DLL3-targeting therapies (tarlatamab shows activity in brain metastases)

supportive_care.evidence: Cancer cachexia affects 50-80% of advanced lung cancer patients. ESPEN guidelines recommend early nutritional assessment and intervention. High Ki-67 tumors drive increased metabolic demands
supportive_care.benefit: Maintains body weight and lean muscle mass during chemotherapy. Improves treatment tolerance and quality of life. Reduces treatment-related complications. Protein and calorie supplementation. Exercise when possible. Consider appetite stimulants (megestrol, olanzapine) if needed

supportive_care.evidence: NCCN Distress Management Guidelines recommend screening at each visit. LCNEC diagnosis in a 45-year-old carries significant psychological burden due to rare diagnosis, aggressive course, and poor prognosis. Depression prevalence 20-40% in advanced lung cancer
supportive_care.benefit: Improved treatment adherence, reduced anxiety and depression, better quality of life. Cognitive behavioral therapy and pharmacotherapy for adjustment disorders. Support groups for rare cancer patients. Family counseling and caregiver support

supportive_care.approach: Bone Health and Skeletal Event Prevention
supportive_care.evidence: Stage IVB metastatic disease has high risk of skeletal-related events. ASCO guidelines recommend denosumab or zoledronic acid for bone metastases. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation for all patients on systemic therapy
supportive_care.benefit: Prevents pathologic fractures, spinal cord compression, and bone pain. Denosumab preferred for convenience (subcutaneous) and renal safety. May delay skeletal-related events by months. Dental evaluation prior to initiation
